profile.summary: A Junior Product Designer helps design digital product flows, screens, and interactions under guidance from more experienced designers. They usually own small features or parts of a journey, turn requirements into wireframes and UI, support research and testing, and work closely with product managers, engineers, and senior designers while building craft, speed, and confidence.

known_challenges.text: Turning vague product requirements into clear design work without over-relying on others for every step.
Balancing speed with quality while still learning core interaction, visual, and systems skills.
Handling frequent critique without taking feedback personally or losing confidence.
Understanding technical constraints and product trade-offs well enough to make practical design choices.
Getting enough context and ownership to grow, instead of only polishing small UI tasks.

recommended_question_themes.why: Junior designers do better when they know what they own, what good looks like, and where to ask for help.
They are still forming judgment, so the way critique is given strongly affects learning and morale.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Growth in core product design craft
recommended_question_themes.why: Progress usually depends on strengthening basics like flows, hierarchy, interaction patterns, and rationale.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Cross-functional collaboration with product and engineering
recommended_question_themes.why: Early habits in working with PMs and engineers shape how practical and effective their design work becomes.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Exposure to users and product context
recommended_question_themes.why: Seeing research, support signals, and outcomes helps them move beyond surface-level UI decisions.

terminology.meaning: A simple layout showing structure before visual polish.
Steps a user takes to complete a task.
Detailed screen design close to the final product.
Clickable model used to test or explain interactions.
Shared components and rules for consistent product design.
Designing so more people can use the product.
Structured feedback on work to improve decisions.
Sharing design details with engineers for build.
Improving a design through repeated changes and feedback.
Learning about user needs before deciding solutions.
Less common scenario the design still needs to handle.
Using AI to generate options or speed early thinking.
Clear explanation of why a design choice was made.
What is gained and lost between design options.
A limit that shapes what solution is practical.
Regular cycle of showing work, getting input, and improving it.

listen_for: Whether they can explain the problem behind the screen, not just the UI they made.
Whether they talk about feedback as learning or mainly as approval-seeking.
How often they mention engineers, PMs, or users when describing design decisions.
Signs they are stuck waiting for direction instead of taking a first pass.
Whether they use design system and accessibility language naturally or avoid it.
avoid: Treating them like a pixel-polisher with no access to problem context.
Giving vague critique like "make it cleaner" without teaching the reasoning.
Mistaking quietness or caution for low potential.
Pushing full ownership too early without support, review, and clear boundaries.
